# Assessing diet and talking to patients

> *Before you can advise anyone, you have to find out what they actually eat — and open the
> conversation without making them defensive. This chapter is the **first contact**: take a
> quick diet history, read a label, and say the first useful sentence, safely.*

## Where the practice begins

The *What a Healthy Diet Looks Like* chapter answered "what should people eat?". This one answers
the question that comes first in real life: **what do they eat now, and how do I find out?** You
cannot counsel a diet you have not assessed, and the same short conversation that surfaces food
also surfaces drinking, activity, sleep and stress — so a diet history is really one branch of a
whole lifestyle history, and the skill transfers everywhere.

It is a skill worth practising early. Graduates consistently report feeling *least* prepared in
exactly this applied, talking-to-patients territory [@crowley2019; @aclm2022comp]. So we start now,
at the right altitude: the aim is **competent first contact** — take a basic history, read a label,
flag the likely problems, and open the conversation — not specialist assessment or a therapeutic
plan. Where a plan turns detailed or restrictive, you *recognise* it and refer.

::: {.callout-note}
## From auditing your own plate to reading someone else's
Part I invited you to audit your *own* diet. The move now is outward: the same structure — a
typical day, the drinks, the portions, who cooks — applied to a patient who is not you, may not
want to talk about it, and will only tell you what you ask.
:::

## How to find out what someone eats

There is no single best method — there is a *fit-for-purpose* one. Three standard tools, each with
a characteristic bias you should be able to name [@measurementToolkitDiet]:

| Method | What it is | Strength | Its signature bias |
|---|---|---|---|
| **24-hour recall** | "Tell me everything you ate and drank yesterday." | Quick, low burden for the patient | **One day is not a usual day** — huge day-to-day variation; also relies on memory (recall bias). Several non-consecutive recalls estimate habitual intake far better. |
| **Food-frequency questionnaire** | A checklist: how often, over months, do you eat X? | Captures the **habitual pattern**; low burden | Best for **ranking** people (high vs low), not for measuring exact quantities; portion estimation is rough. |
| **Food diary** | The patient records everything as they eat it, for several days | The **richest** detail; no memory error | High burden; **reactivity** — people eat differently *because* they are recording; and they under-report the "bad" items. |

Two honest caveats sit over all of them. First, self-reported intake is **systematically
under-reported** — people leave out or shrink the less-flattering foods and portions, and
under-reporting is greater at higher body weight [@measurementToolkitDiet]. Second, the fix is not
suspicion but **manner**: ask in a way that does not invite the "right" answer (more on that
below). For a first clinic visit, a short structured history — essentially a guided 24-hour recall
widened to "is that typical?" — is usually the fit-for-purpose choice.

## Taking a diet history

A diet history is a **structured conversation, not an interrogation**. Walk through a typical day
from waking to sleeping, then probe the high-yield areas: meal pattern and skipping, **drinks**,
snacks, how often they eat out or *tapau*, portion sizes, who shops and cooks, and supplements.
Keep it conversational; let the facts come out rather than firing questions.

One probe outyields all the others locally: **drinks**. Sugary drinks — *kopi* and *teh* with
condensed milk, *bandung*, bubble tea, canned soft drinks — are the biggest hidden source of energy
and free sugar in many Singaporean diets, and they are the question most often skipped. The drinks
probe also folds in a routine, non-judgemental **alcohol** question. Try the history below: click
the questions you would ask and watch the picture build — and notice what you miss if you never ask
what she drinks.

<div data-island="diet-history" data-src="/diagrams/diet-history.yml"></div>

::: {.callout-tip}
## The highest-yield question is often the one not asked
People rarely volunteer their drinks, their snacks, or their true portions — not to deceive, but
because they "don't count." Ask specifically, with everyday anchors ("how big — a *rice bowl*? two
scoops?"), and you uncover far more than an open "so, how's your diet?" ever will.
:::

## Reading a food label

The *What a Healthy Diet Looks Like* chapter covered *why* the food environment matters — the
policy and the framing. Here is the **skill**: actually reading what is in front of a patient.

On a packaged **Nutrition Information Panel**, the trap is the **serving size**. A figure is quoted
"per serving," but the serving may be small or unrealistic — a bottle labelled "2.5 servings" hides
how much sugar you drink when you finish it in one go. Always check the **per-100 g / per-100 ml**
column, which lets you compare products fairly, and glance at the **ingredient list** (ordered by
weight — sugar near the top under any of its many names is a signal).

### Nutri-Grade: grade the drink, pick a swap

Singapore's front-of-pack **Nutri-Grade** mark turns the label into a one-glance judgement for
*drinks*. It grades a beverage **A to D** by **sugar per 100 ml** — **A ≤1 g, B >1–5 g, C >5–10 g,
D >10 g** — and then **downgrades** it if its **saturated fat** is high, so a low-sugar but creamy
drink still cannot reach an A. Grades **C and D must display the mark; grade D cannot be
advertised**. Crucially, since **30 December 2023** the mark covers **freshly-prepared** drinks —
*kopi*, *teh*, and bubble tea at the stall — not just supermarket cartons (pre-packaged drinks have
carried it since 30 December 2022) [@hpbNutriGrade]. That makes "**grade your usual drink, then pick
one swap**" an authentic local exercise:

<div data-island="nutri-grade" data-src="/diagrams/nutri-grade.yml"></div>

Teach it as a **nudge, not a verdict**. In a randomised trial of the label, shoppers bought drinks
with meaningfully **less sugar** (about 1.5 g less per serving) — but the label alone did **not**
improve the overall quality of their basket [@nutriGradeRCT2023]. A grade moves one choice; it does
not fix a diet. And it grades only *drinks* on *two* nutrients — an "A" drink is not a licence, and
Nutri-Grade says nothing about solid food.

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Eating out: the one swap that lands
The single highest-yield swap in a Singapore diet history is the drink. *Kopi-o kosong* or
*teh-o kosong* (grade A) instead of the sweetened default; **"siu dai"** (less sweet) or
**"kosong"** (no sugar) when you order; water instead of the bundled soft drink; whole fruit
instead of a pressed juice. One realistic swap the patient agrees to beats a lecture on all of them.
:::

## From the history to the likely problems

A history is only useful if it points somewhere. Link the **pattern** to the **likely surplus and
shortfall**. Locally the surpluses are predictable — **sodium** (instant noodles, soup
bases, and sauces on everything), **free sugars** (those drinks), and **saturated fat** (coconut
gravy, fried food, fatty and processed meat) — because national data show most residents exceed the
sodium
recommendation and very little of the staple eaten is wholegrain [@mohNNS2022]. The shortfalls
follow the same logic: **wholegrains, fibre, fruit and vegetables**, and in some groups **calcium**
or **iron**. You are not calculating nutrient intakes; you are reading a pattern and naming, in
plain terms, the two or three things worth shifting.

## Weight, screening and both tails of malnutrition

Anthropometry adds an objective anchor. **Body mass index** classifies weight-for-height, but the
thresholds are not universal: Singapore uses **Asian cut-offs** — **overweight at BMI ≥23** and
**obese at BMI ≥27.5** kg/m² — because cardiometabolic risk rises at a lower BMI in Asian
populations than the international ≥25/≥30 [@sgObesityBMI]. A **waist circumference** ≥90 cm (men) or
≥80 cm (women) flags central adiposity. BMI is a screen, not a verdict — it misses body composition
(a muscular person, an older person who has lost muscle) — so read it alongside the history, not
instead of it.

The commoner instinct is to look only at the *high* tail. But some patients are **under-nourished**
— an unwell older adult who has lost weight and appetite, someone eating alone and skipping meals.
A validated tool such as the **Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool (MUST)** — which combines BMI,
recent **unplanned weight loss**, and any acute illness stopping intake — flags this risk and
triggers a referral pathway [@bapenMUST]. At this stage you **recognise when it applies and refer**;
you do not need to score and manage it. (The recognition and management of the malnourished patient
is the work of the undernutrition chapter.)

::: {.callout-important}
## Always consider both tails
"Lower on the scale is better" is a dangerous default. Before you suggest anyone eat *less*, ask
whether the real problem is that they are eating *too little* — and whether weight talk is safe at
all (the next section).
:::

## Talking to patients: the first moves

Knowing the food is half the job; the other half is the conversation, and it has structure. The
widely recommended scaffold for brief counselling is the **5 A's** — **Assess, Advise, Agree,
Assist, Arrange** [@fiveAsNutrition]. The full cycle, and the motivational-interviewing skills that
power it, belong to the *Behaviour-Change Counselling* chapter. Here you practise only the **opening
moves**, because they are where first contact succeeds or fails:

- **Assess** — find out what they eat *and* what they are ready to change, without judgement. This
  is the diet history you just took.
- **Advise** — offer **one** concrete, realistic step, **with permission**. "Would it be alright if
  I shared one idea about your drinks?" earns a hearing; an unasked-for lecture does not.

Two rules carry most of the weight. **Ask before you advise** — permission turns advice into a
conversation. And **use plain language** — "sugary drinks", not "free sugars exceeding ten percent
of energy". Brief, respectful counselling in this style measurably improves what people actually eat
[@frost2018mi]; hectoring does not.

## Do no harm

Food, weight and "clean eating" talk can **trigger or worsen disordered eating**, and
weight-management advice itself carries a measurable eating-disorder risk in vulnerable people
[@jebeile2023edrisk]. So a do-no-harm reflex belongs at first contact, *before* any advice to
restrict. Listen for red flags — skipping meals to "make up" for eating, guilt or secrecy around
food, a driven pursuit of thinness, weight talk that feels charged.

When something flags, a brief screen such as **SCOFF** (five questions on vomiting, loss of control,
recent weight loss, feeling fat, and food dominating life) raises or lowers suspicion — pooled
sensitivity about 0.86 and specificity about 0.83 [@scoffMorgan1999; @kutzScoff2020]. But it is a
**screen, not a diagnosis**: a positive means *ask more and consider referral*, and a **negative
does not exclude** an eating disorder. The safe play at this stage is the same either way — do not
prescribe restriction into a possible eating disorder; be supportive, and refer.

## The Singapore picture

Everything here has a local hook. Nutri-Grade is uniquely useful because it now reaches the
**freshly-prepared** drinks people actually buy — the bubble tea and the *kopi* — so "grade your
drink" is a real exercise, not a supermarket abstraction [@hpbNutriGrade]. The National Nutrition
Survey tells you what to *expect* before the patient opens their mouth: high sodium, sweet drinks,
refined staples, too little wholegrain [@mohNNS2022]. And the whole approach fits the national
**preventive-care** direction — screening and a short conversation at first contact, then referral
where needed — rather than waiting for disease.

## So — how do you start?

You start by **asking, not advising**. Take a quick, structured history — and make sure you ask
about the drinks. Read the label with the patient: check per-100 ml, grade the drink, name one swap.
Place the findings against both tails of malnutrition, using the Asian BMI cut-offs and recognising
when a screening tool is indicated. Then say one useful, permission-first sentence — and, when the
picture is complex, restrictive, or a red flag shows, **recognise the boundary and refer**. That is
first contact done well; how to carry the conversation all the way to change is the
*Behaviour-Change Counselling* chapter.

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/06-dietary-assessment.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: weight gain and bubble tea

A 24-year-old office worker, overweight by the Asian cut-off, has gained weight over the past year
and asks you for "a diet". Take a history, read the drink, open the conversation with
permission — and stay alert for the signal that this is not a weight-loss conversation at all. Your
choices branch the case.

::: {.callout-tip}
Progress is saved locally — replay to explore the other paths.
:::

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/weight-gain-bubble-tea.case.yml"></div>
